speaker: interviewee
text: Myself not to cry. I cried enough--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're allowed. You're allowed. You have reason to cry. So don't-- don't feel that you have to be a shtarker. If you want to take a minute or two, and-- it's OK. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You're a strong lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 26, 1995. The survivor is Chana Gitalis. The interviewer is Fran Star. We are in Toronto, Canada, and the interview will be in English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is an interview with Chana Gitalis. Today is May 26, 1995. I am Fran Star, the interviewer. We are in the city of Toronto, Canada, and this will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Could you tell us your first and last name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Chana Gitalis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Gitalis?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-T-A-L-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Melamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-L-A-M-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you-- when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: June the 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: June the 10th, 1920? And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Polish Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like for you growing up in Luboml?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little city, a lot of Jews. We went to Hebrew school, to Polish school. Like in-- in the day time, we went to school, to Polish school. And Sundays, we went to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the percentage of Jewish people in public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was half and half. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get along with the gentile people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. We lived on a street with the Ukrainian people, and they were very good neighbors before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you socialize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of activities would you do with the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like with the Jewish children, we went to [INAUDIBLE], it was an organization. We had ver-- very good times together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you socialize with gentile people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- they lived their life, and we lived our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during your time going to school, was there any anti-semitism that you noticed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One respect-- respect each other. Like in our street, no trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father sold meat, and my mother was a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I had two sisters and one brother. One sister was married. She was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Hinda, and my other sister was Esther, and my brother was Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that Hinda was married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hinda was married in-- to Kowel. Kowel was a bigger city than ours. She had two children. Her husband's name was Aaron Fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Esther and Samuel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't. They have been married after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about your upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] I remember Saturday, Friday night. My father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came from the synagogue. He made Kiddush and we had a beautiful supper together. We sung [NON-ENGLISH] at home, all of us. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the synagogue was-- I really don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi was also Malamed. His name was Malamed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leibel Melamed, this I remember good. He lived near the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did life revolve around activities at the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much. Like, everyone lived by themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would your family do as family outings together?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Saturday we were together. Like, everyday life was like we went to school, and when we came at night, my father ask us about everything about school. My brother went to Hebrew school all the time. And it was-- was nice. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that you especially liked doing? Did you sew? Did you read? Did you play an instrument?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We just-- there wasn't the time. Like, we were-- we came from school, so we made the lessons and-- but it was very nice. We-- I had very good friends. We went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with your sister and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. I used to go to Kowel very often to my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And visit with her family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came to us. It was a very nice, quiet life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. I was engaged to be married.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Symcha Gitalis. He was also from my city. I used to come in into the house to his sister. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister's name was [? Hamka. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hamka. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Gitalis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were originally friends with the sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends with the sister and then we-- we went together and I was engaged to marry. It was before the war, 1939. And we supposed to be married '41, the war break out, and we lost each other. And we find each other four years later. We married the same month we supposed to.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war broke out, how did that affect you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It surely affect us very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the-- the Russian came to us. They stayed, like, they came '39 and they left in '41, start again with the-- with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war start with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came, was-- it wasn't so bad. It was a different kind of life, but we adjust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Changes like we couldn't have, for our self, business. Everything was liquidated. But we adjust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father lost his work? His job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did the family sustain themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother sewed a little bit and it was very poor. Very poor, very hard. And then the war break out with Hitler. So it was very bad. I went to Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in Kowel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister lived in Kowel and we had my sister's child. So we took him there and my sister didn't let us go to-- back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Kowel with your sister, Hinda--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the rest of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, me and my sister, my younger sister. And my mother and father were in Luboml. Later the German took away my father, so my mother came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were living in a ghetto at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a ghetto then when my mother came. Later, in-- three months later, it was a-- they-- we had a ghetto, Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in Kowel, and-- did you go back to Luboml, or you stayed in Kowel the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. We couldn't go back. No. We stayed in Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my sister, my mother, stayed together with my sister-- my older sister, Hinda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with the family when--

speaker: interviewee
text: The family-- the-- we-- they took out the people sometimes. We-- they made a ghetto. One ghetto was in-- in old-- in the old city Kowel, and one ghetto was in the new city Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the streets where the ghettos were?

speaker: interviewee
text: One ghetto was on the [NON-ENGLISH] Street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other ghetto was on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- it said in-- on [INAUDIBLE]. They called the other side the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you have to move from your sister's home to move into the ghetto, or was that part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We all-- we all-- we all have to move. They-- I-- I used to work. I work with the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked. They took us to work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a-- [GERMAN]. It was on the-- where the trains are-- on the-- oh, what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: The railway tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The railway, yeah. We worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were allowed to leave the ghetto to go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they took us. They took us to the ghetto. We couldn't go by themself. Like, 50 people. They came in and they took us, and they bri-- brought us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of boundaries did the ghetto have? Were there fences or walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a fence. Yeah. And the police was watching. We couldn't go out. If somebody went out, they killed them right away. We had to stay in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of these killings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- oh, yes. One day they-- they called every-- every Jew was supposed to be-- supposed to come to the Judenrat. It was like a city hall. And the-- the people, they worked in the Judenrat. The-- the German told them to come out. And all the people-- you should see-- they took the revolvers and they kill all the five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which five people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They work. They work in the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like this, what they killed them. They killed Jews then. And then they say to a-- to a-- one Jew, they say to them, bring me water. I want to wash my-- my hands from the dirty Jews I killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the community--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the community react to these killings?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say anything. Everyone was afraid to say something. You didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have a place to escape. All over was police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life in the ghetto was difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? It wasn't-- we-- we had just half a-- half a pound bread for each person. So you have to stay in the line until we get the bread. But the people who worked, so it was easier. Sometimes they get, from other people, bread they brought. It wasn't for a long time. But they didn't have where to take the bread. It's not--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were other conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't describe the life in the ghetto. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. We-- we lived in a-- in a little room, about five families together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 10 people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there washing facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was very bad. We can't describe that. But we got through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every day you got up and you went to work on the railway tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time would you get back in the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked the whole day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got back to your family in the evening, what did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We cried, all of us. Everyone tells a-- told another story, what's-- what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on in the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no radios or no--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work on the railway tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been in-- I've been in ghetto one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they used to-- when they killed, in the other ghetto, the people, they took us out from their houses and they killed them. The name of the place was [NON-ENGLISH] in Kowel. So in one day, they killed my mother, my brother-in-law, my sister and the two children. And-- and me and my sister were together in the other ghetto because we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people came. A lot of people came. They run away, so they came to us and they told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, how did people react?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and left other people that there was hide there. They couldn't find them. They find them in a few days and they brought-- they brought the people to us. So they told us what's what was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you fear that the same thing would happen in the ghetto you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did people do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't do anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- we were-- we were last. We knew it will come a day and we'll go. But my-- my sister has a boy-- had a boyfriend, and he worked not far from Kowel. It was Kosz-- the name of the place was Koszary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did he do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked whatever he had, outside on the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he made, like-- he helped one man make the-- the fence, like some-- some things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this boyfriend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motl Fisher. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he heard about everyone was killed. So he took a man, he paid him, and he came into the ghetto. He came into the ghetto, but the people, which they took him out for work, he went in with them and and he took out my sister with a man who should bring the bread, what the Germans-- they gave him the bread for the people. He went-- they went with him out of-- of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motl Fisher was able to sneak your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the ghetto. And they went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did this because he knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew they're going to kill us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he knew the man who delivered the bread to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We paid him. I had my-- my-- my Singer machine, so I gave him the Singer machine away and he did it for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to keep your Singer machine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had it in the room. We brought this when we went to-- to the ghetto. We took just the head from the machine, and the other left-- and the other half we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other items that you took to the ghetto with you that have--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't. Just clothes. We couldn't took too much. They didn't let us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in exchange for the Singer sewing machine--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This man offered--

speaker: interviewee
text: He took away my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: To sneak your sister out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take her?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very-- it was very [INAUDIBLE]. It was very hard to go out, but we sur-- we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of this man who delivered the bread to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I remember the man, but I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came and they took your sister out of the ghetto. Where did they take her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take-- they took her to the Koszary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Koszary?

speaker: interviewee
text: A man was waiting with a-- with a-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it was a man who was waiting, and he took us. Not a Jewish man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they took us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Took them-- my brother-in-law and my sister. And I was-- and I stayed in the ghetto. And the other day, my brother-in-law came to me to take me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days later?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day. The ne-- the next day he came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Motl Fisher came back to the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went out with the people, we sneak in with the people they took to the work. And the people, they-- they-- they went to-- with a-- with a train, they went to work. It's the same way we supposed to go, to the Koszary. They took them to Koszary to work. So we sneak in with the people. They took to work, me and my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you sneak in? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- we went-- this was in ghetto, when they took the people from ghetto. They came to the ghetto to take the people, two policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sneak in with the people. They have to-- they have to count every day the people who-- how much they take. After the-- they count the people, we sneak in, both of us. And we went together. They went on the train on another side, and we supposed to go in the other side-- in the other side on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 20 minutes by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the train stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the train-- the train-- before she stopped. When she stopped, we couldn't go-- we couldn't go. When the train didn't go so fast, we jumped, me and my brother-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were very-- I-- I couldn't stand up because it was very-- I was very-- I was shocked and hurt from the train. We didn't go-- we rolled on the-- on the-- from the train. And then we-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you lie there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you lie on the side of the railway track?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe half an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you not afraid that people were going to come looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we didn't know what's going on. We were in shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you finally managed to get up and move, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went a few kilometers, the place where the-- the-- the man, the Russian man, wait for us to take us with.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Russian man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met up with the Russian man, and where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a place where the-- my brother-in-law has the place we were supposed to be. It was-- he was the-- the [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the little town. He was the-- he-- he was the-- the-- he did everything for the-- for the town, this man. So he gave us a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Koszary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of place did he offer you? Was it a barn? Was it in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first few days, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They gave us food. Yeah. He worked, so he had food. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he helped you, this Russian man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- they-- they are-- the-- the Russian are good men too, good people too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he expect anything back in return?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he gave him, yeah. He paid him. He paid him. My brother-in-law gave him money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spent a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spent a few days there and then we couldn't stay there because all the people knew we are newcomers, and we couldn't stay. And some of them were afraid because we are Jewish. So we have to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we hide. We went to-- to the bushes. We hide in the bushes every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was you and your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me-- no, my brother-in-law worked, so he ca-- he could work. They didn't say anything. But me and my sister, we didn't want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have to go every time to-- to hide in places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Motl Fisher come back and see you every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he'd come back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And check up on you and Esther.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you hide in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then they came-- the police came-- and they said to the-- to the man with my brother-in-law worked, to give the-- the two sisters. We were supposed to go with them, with the police. But he didn't. He said we went away. We are not here. We run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you and your sister were hiding for two months in the forest, how did you survive? Where did you get your food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in the-- in the bushes were-- it was nuts, little nuts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the black nuts. So we ate them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sometimes. Yeah, when we came with my brother-in-law, he brought us bread. Yeah. We weren't-- every-- I wasn't hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you run into anybody else who was hiding in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of anybody else who would help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No one could help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, you relied on Motl Fisher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And then, they-- in a few weeks later, they said to-- to the man who my brother-in-law worked, he's not supposed to work. They're going to kill him. Take him away. He has to go away. He has to go away because they're going to kill them. So we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was going to kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who told him that he was going to be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian-- the Russian-- the police came. So we-- we hide. We went to Tmosz Gerasimuk. Tmosz. His name was Tmosz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tmosz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tmosz Gerasimuk. And we ask him, maybe he's going to take us to our city, Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know this Tmosz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my brother-in-law knew him very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: He told him-- he told them he is hiding a woman with two children from Kowel. So he knew if he-- he tell him something, he won't tell anyone. So he came to-- my brother-in-law came to him and he asked him if he can take him-- him and us to-- back to Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his daughter was-- was with Tmosz Gerasimuk. So she said, don't take him. Don't take him to Luboml. You come to us, and we will-- we will take care of you, you and your little sisters. So we did. That [INAUDIBLE] for two years. What was the daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they agree to hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were good people. They were angels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they expect anything back in return?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't give them anything. Later on, we ga-- we did. But we didn't have anything. They hide us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why do you think he hid you? Why would he risk his life to hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- it's a miracle. We didn't know him before. It is a miracle he did for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll go to break now.
